name: Possessive Pronouns
summary: Show ownership with ko, mo, niya, and their fronted forms akin, iyo, and kaniya.
explanation:
Filipino has two related sets of possessives. The first set, the ng set, comes after the thing owned: ko (my), mo (your), niya (his/her), namin (our, not you), natin (our, and you), ninyo (your, plural/polite), and nila (their). So aklat ko means my book, and bahay niya means his/her house.

The second set is the fronted or sa set, used when the possessive comes first or stands alone for emphasis: akin (mine), iyo (yours), kaniya (his/hers), amin, atin, inyo, kanila. Akin ang aklat means the book is mine.

When ko or mo follow a word ending in a vowel or n, they often attach with a linker (aklat ko stays separate, but gusto ko + ng becomes gusto kong). The same kami/tayo distinction from pronouns applies here: namin excludes the listener, natin includes them. Pick the set by position: after the noun use ko/mo/niya, at the front use akin/iyo/kaniya.
pattern: after noun: ko / mo / niya | fronted: akin / iyo / kaniya
commonMistakes: Using akin after a noun (aklat akin) instead of the correct aklat ko.
Using ko at the front of a sentence; the fronted form is akin.
Confusing namin (excludes listener) with natin (includes listener).
Forgetting that niya covers both his and her with no gender split.
Adding ang before ko or mo; the ng-set possessives attach directly to the noun.
tip: Position decides the set: trailing the noun, use ko/mo/niya; leading or standing alone, use akin/iyo/kaniya.
